root.narration: Bottom of the seventh -- the last inning in your league. You're down by one with a runner on third and one out. Your runner on third is your team captain -- he's smart, reads pitchers well, and he's got decent speed. You're at the plate with a .260 average. Not a slugger, not a strikeout machine. The infield is playing at normal depth. Your coach is about to give you a sign. This run TIES the game. How do you want to bring it home?

decision-1.narration: Your coach calls time and jogs over. 'What do you think? Suicide squeeze, safety squeeze, or do you want to swing away?' He's actually asking YOUR opinion here. This is a massive moment -- the wrong call ends the game. The right one ties it up. The pitcher throws hard but he's been wild today -- two walks this inning. The third baseman is playing back. What's your call?

choices.text: Suicide squeeze! The runner breaks on the pitch and I bunt it on the ground. High risk, high reward -- if I get it down, the run scores for sure!
Safety squeeze. I'll bunt, but the runner waits to see if I get it down before he commits. Safer for both of us.
Swing away! I want to hit a sac fly or drive the ball. A bunt is too risky with the game on the line.

transition-suicide-pitch.narration: Suicide squeeze is ON! Your runner knows the sign. On this pitch, he's breaking for home the instant the pitcher commits to the plate. Everything -- EVERYTHING -- depends on you getting the bunt down on the ground. The pitcher comes set, starts his delivery, and your runner GOES! The pitch is coming in high and tight -- up near your hands! This is NOT a good pitch to bunt!

decision-suicide.narration: The pitch is high and inside -- tough to bunt! But your runner is already committed, sprinting home. He can't stop. He's a dead duck if you don't bunt this ball. What do you do?

choices.text: Get the bunt down no matter what! Even a bad bunt is better than no bunt -- the runner is dead if I pull back!
That pitch is too high to bunt -- I have to pull back and protect myself.
Forget the bunt -- I'm going to swing at it and try to drive the runner in!

outcome-suicide-bunt-great.narration: You stab the bat up at the high pitch and manage to deaden it! The bunt isn't pretty -- it dribbles toward the pitcher -- but the ball is ON THE GROUND and that's all that matters! The pitcher has to stop, field the ball, and by the time he picks it up and looks home, your runner is SLIDING across the plate! TIE GAME! The pitcher throws to first and gets you out, but who cares -- THE RUN SCORED! Your whole dugout erupts! You sacrificed yourself to tie the game, and that's exactly what heroes do!

outcome.headline: SQUEEZE SCORES THE RUN! TIE GAME!
outcome.explanation: This is the art of the suicide squeeze: once the runner commits, your ONLY job is to get the bunt on the ground. It doesn't have to be a good bunt. It doesn't have to be pretty. It just needs to hit the dirt. Here's the math: from third base, a runner at full sprint reaches home plate in about 3.3 seconds. The pitcher has to field the bunt, set his feet, and throw. That takes at LEAST 2-3 seconds on a ball hit near the mound. Even a terrible bunt that rolls right back to the pitcher usually scores the run because the runner had such a huge head start. The suicide squeeze is baseball's most exciting play because when you commit, there's no turning back. You committed, and you delivered.
outcome.whatToRemember: On a suicide squeeze, get the bunt down NO MATTER WHAT. Ugly bunt? Fine. Dribbler to the pitcher? Fine. The runner is coming and the ball must hit the ground. Any bunt on the ground scores the run.

outcome-suicide-pullback-bad.narration: You pull back the bat -- it IS a ball, high and inside. But LOOK AT THIRD BASE! Your runner is halfway home! He's COMMITTED! The catcher catches the ball and your runner is hung out to dry in no-man's land. The catcher takes two steps and tags him easily. He's OUT. Your runner just stares at you as he walks back to the dugout. The tying run was just erased because you didn't hold up your end of the deal. Two outs now. The go-ahead run was right there, 90 feet away, and now he's sitting on the bench.

outcome.headline: Runner Tagged Out! You Abandoned the Play!
outcome.explanation: This is the nightmare scenario of the suicide squeeze, and it happened because you didn't understand the most important word in 'suicide squeeze': SUICIDE. The runner committed his entire existence on this play to YOU. He left third base the moment the pitcher started his delivery. He CANNOT go back. He is running no matter what. When you don't bunt, the catcher catches the ball and tags your runner out standing up. It's not even close. Here's the iron law: on a suicide squeeze, you bunt. Period. Even if the pitch is at your head, you try to get the bat on it. Even a foul bunt gives the runner a chance to retreat. Pulling back completely leaves him with zero chance.
outcome.whatToRemember: NEVER pull back on a suicide squeeze. The runner is committed. If you don't bunt, he's dead. Even a foul ball is better than pulling back. The word 'suicide' means total commitment from both players.

outcome-suicide-swing-bad.narration: You swing at the high inside pitch and foul it straight back into the screen. Strike one. Your runner -- who was sprinting home -- has to slam on the brakes. He tries to scramble back to third but the foul ball saved him. He gets back safely, but barely. Your coach is NOT happy. The sign was bunt, not swing. You got lucky with the foul -- if you had missed entirely, the catcher guns the runner. If you hit a line drive, your runner could have been doubled off. The squeeze play requires a BUNT, not a swing.

outcome.headline: Wrong Play! Squeeze Means BUNT!
outcome.explanation: You got bailed out by the foul ball, but this was a dangerous mistake. On a squeeze play, swinging is the wrong action for multiple reasons: 1) If you swing and miss, the catcher has the ball and your runner is toast. At least with a bunt attempt and miss, your bat angle might interfere with the catcher. 2) If you hit a line drive, the fielder catches it and your runner -- who left early -- gets doubled off third base. That's a double play and the inning is over. 3) If you hit a ground ball, the infielder might turn two because the runner is going on contact anyway. The bunt is called for a reason: a ball on the ground close to home plate gives the defense the LEAST time to react. A swing introduces too many bad outcomes.
outcome.whatToRemember: Squeeze play = bunt. Always. Swinging on a squeeze is dangerous: line drives become double plays, swings-and-misses expose the runner, and ground balls to infielders can turn two. The bunt keeps the ball close to home where the defense has the least time.

transition-safety-pitch.narration: Safety squeeze it is! The runner knows the deal: he stays close to third base and only breaks for home AFTER you get the bunt down on the ground. If you miss, he stays put. If you pop it up, he stays put. Only a bunt on the dirt triggers the run. The pitcher checks the runner and delivers -- it's a fastball, middle of the plate, belt-high. That's a GREAT pitch to bunt!

decision-safety.narration: The pitch is right down the middle -- perfect to bunt! Your runner is watching from third, ready to sprint home if you get it down. Where do you try to place the bunt?

choices.text: Bunt it down the third-base line! The third baseman is playing back -- he'll have a long throw!
Bunt it toward first base! Push it past the pitcher on the right side.
Just deaden it -- I don't care where it goes, I just want it on the ground.

outcome-safety-third-great.narration: You angle the bat perfectly and the bunt rolls down the third-base line like it's on a rail! The third baseman was playing BACK -- he charges in, but by the time he bare-hands the ball, your runner is already rounding the line and heading home! The third baseman has to make a long, off-balance throw... he throws to first to get YOU out, because the play at home is too late! RUN SCORES! TIE GAME! Sac bunt, one out, but who cares -- you moved from down one to TIED with a perfectly placed safety squeeze!

outcome.headline: Safety Squeeze Perfection! Run Scores!
outcome.explanation: Beautiful execution! Here's why the third-base line was the perfect target: 1) The third baseman was playing BACK at normal depth, so he had to charge a long way to field the bunt. 2) Once he commits forward to field the ball, there's nobody covering third and the throw home is long and off-balance. 3) He's forced to take the out at first instead of trying to get the runner -- it's the safer play for the defense, and that's GREAT for you because the run scores! The safety squeeze gave your runner the best of both worlds: he could read the bunt before committing, and your placement made it impossible for the defense to get the run.
outcome.whatToRemember: On a safety squeeze, bunt toward the third-base line when the third baseman is playing back. He has the longest distance to charge and the hardest throw. The run almost always scores.

outcome-safety-first-good.narration: You push the bunt toward the first-base side! It rolls past the pitcher and the first baseman has to come in to field it. Your runner sees the bunt hit the ground and TAKES OFF for home! The first baseman fields the ball, looks home -- the runner is close! He decides to eat the ball and take the out at first instead... wait, nobody's covering first! The pitcher runs over to cover, the first baseman flips to him, and you're out. But the RUN SCORES! Tie game! It worked, but it wasn't clean -- if the first baseman had gone home instead, it would've been a bang-bang play.

outcome.headline: Run Scores! But First-Base Side Was Risky.
outcome.explanation: The run scored, so this is a success! But bunting to the first-base side on a safety squeeze is riskier than the third-base side. Here's why: the first baseman is typically playing CLOSER to home plate than the third baseman in a bunt situation, so he gets to the ball faster. Also, his throw home is shorter and on a straight line. If the first baseman had been more aggressive, he might have thrown home and nailed your runner. The third-base line is almost always the better target on a squeeze because: the third baseman plays deeper, his charge to the ball is longer, and his throw home is at a bad angle. You got the job done, but the third-base side would have been a higher-percentage play.
outcome.whatToRemember: On a squeeze bunt, the third-base line is usually safer than the first-base line. The third baseman plays deeper, charges farther, and has a harder throw home. First-base side can work but it's a coin flip.

outcome-safety-dead-okay.narration: You deaden the ball right in front of home plate -- it dies about five feet in front of the catcher. The catcher pounces on it immediately. Your runner was reading the bunt and started home, but he sees the catcher pick it up RIGHT THERE and stops! He's stuck! He retreats back to third just barely in time. The catcher fires to first to get you out. Two outs now, runner still on third. You burned an out and the runner didn't score. Not a disaster, but the bunt was too soft and too close to the catcher.

outcome.headline: Runner Held. Bunt Too Soft.
outcome.explanation: On a safety squeeze, placement matters more than anything. You got the bunt down -- that's good! But it died right in front of the catcher, which is the WORST place it could go. The catcher barely had to move. He grabbed it instantly and the runner -- smartly using the 'safety' part of the safety squeeze -- saw that the catcher had the ball and stopped. At least he didn't get thrown out! The lesson: on a squeeze bunt, you need to push the ball AWAY from the catcher. Down the third-base line or past the pitcher toward first. The goal is to make a fielder run toward the ball, giving your runner time to score. A bunt that stays near home plate defeats the entire purpose.
outcome.whatToRemember: On a squeeze, push the bunt AWAY from the catcher. A bunt that dies at the catcher's feet is the worst outcome. Aim down a baseline to force a fielder to charge the ball and make a long throw.

transition-swing-pitch.narration: You decide to swing away! Your coach nods -- he trusts your bat. The pitcher delivers... it's a fastball, up in the zone but hittable. You take a hack!

decision-swing-result.narration: You swing! The ball is hit in the air to medium-depth center field. The center fielder settles under it. Your runner is tagging up at third base. The catch is made -- that's two outs now. Does your runner tag and go for home?

choices.text: Go! Tag up and run! The center fielder has a long throw -- we can make it!
Hold! It's too shallow -- the throw will beat him. Don't risk the last out.

outcome-sac-fly-good.narration: Your runner tags up and races home! The center fielder catches the ball and unleashes a throw toward the plate. It's going to be close... your runner slides... SAFE! TIE GAME! The throw was slightly up the third-base line and the catcher couldn't get the tag down in time! What a play! But look -- you're at two outs now with nobody on base. You tied the game, but a squeeze bunt might have done the same thing with only one out, giving your team more chances to take the lead.

outcome.headline: Sac Fly Ties It! But Two Outs Now.
outcome.explanation: The sac fly worked! Your runner scored and the game is tied. But let's look at the bigger picture: swinging away used two outs to score one run (the fly ball out + you're done). A successful squeeze bunt would have scored that same run using only ONE out, leaving more batters to potentially take the lead. In a tie game with one out, the squeeze play is statistically better because: 1) A good bunt scores the run almost 100% of the time. 2) It only costs one out instead of potentially two. 3) You still have runners coming up to bat. Swinging away has more upside (home run, extra-base hit) but also more downside (strikeout, pop-up, line drive double play). The squeeze is the higher-percentage play with runner on third and less than two outs.
outcome.whatToRemember: Swinging away with a runner on third can work (sac flies!), but a squeeze bunt is usually the higher-percentage play because it uses fewer outs to score the same run. Think efficiency, not power.

outcome-hold-okay.narration: Your runner holds at third. Smart -- the throw did come in pretty quickly and it might have been a close play. But now you've got two outs, runner on third, and the next batter has to get a hit to bring the run home. The pressure shifts to someone else. Your fly ball used up an out without scoring the run. If you had bunted instead, the run very likely would have scored with only one out used.

outcome.headline: Fly Out. Runner Stuck. Pressure Mounts.
outcome.explanation: You chose to swing away and hit a fly ball that was too shallow to score the runner. Now it's two outs with a runner on third -- the next batter absolutely MUST get a hit to score the tying run. Compare this to the squeeze: if you had bunted the ball down the third-base line, the run almost certainly scores, it's only one out, and your team still has more chances to take the lead. Swinging away with a runner on third and one out is a gamble. Sometimes it pays off (home run, deep fly ball, line drive hit). But sometimes you get a shallow fly ball, a strikeout, or a ground ball that doesn't score the run. The squeeze is the steady, reliable play -- and in a do-or-die situation, reliable is usually the smarter choice.
outcome.whatToRemember: With a runner on third and less than two outs, a squeeze bunt is more reliable than swinging away. Fly balls might not be deep enough, and strikeouts score nobody. The bunt puts the ball in play and almost guarantees the run.
